input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: Serial robotic arm MABI MAX 100 (six‑axis, industrial robot from MABI Robotic AG) – the study focuses on its first vertical axis. The axis comprises a permanent‑magnet synchronous motor driving a gear train that includes a cycloidal gearbox, providing high torque accuracy and path repeatability. The robot is deployed in machining and manufacturing tasks, exhibiting flexible rotary joints and precise trajectory execution suitable for digital twin development.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The digital twin implements a passive monitoring and diagnostics service built on digital shadow principles. It continuously ingests joint kinematic and dynamic data from the MABI MAX 100 through an automated one‑way data stream, applies a multi‑physics‑aware identification algorithm to detect motor friction and wear, and visualises these findings in a real‑time dashboard. Users such as operators and maintenance engineers can perform root‑cause analysis of performance degradations, but the twin does not exert any control over the physical robot—the closed‑loop control remains embedded in the robot’s controller.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Built following Model‑Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) principles, the twin’s implementation blends SysML (v1.7 and IBDs) with architecture description languages such as ACME, AADL and MontiArc. Functional and logical behavior is encoded by MontiArc Automaton and SysML state machines, subsequently simulated with MathWorks Simulink. Core data ingestion relies on an automated one‑way data flow consistent with the Digital Shadow reference model, demanding active processing modules. Code generation utilizes C++ or Java for real‑time components. These tools, along with database management layers for state persistence, form the primary enablers for integrating physical data streams with the virtual twin.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Computational models in the Digital Twin are specified using Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) languages such as SysML [17] for functional and logical architecture representation, and component‑and‑connector oriented ADLs (ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25, 26]) for higher‑level structural modeling. Behavioral aspects are captured with state‑machine definitions (MontiArc Automaton or SysML v1.7 state machines) and validated through simulation tools like MathWorks Simulink [28]. The constellation supports heterogeneous modeling notations and integrates code written in general‑purpose languages (C++, Java). Data inputs are sourced from the physical environment via a Digital Shadow reference model (passive one‑way data flow) and aggregated and abstracted within the twin for further analysis and control.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The system captures real‑time sensor time series from the physical asset, specifically torque (τ) and angular velocity (ω), and streams them to the digital twin via the MQTT messaging protocol. The digital shadow acts as a passive receiver, aggregating and abstracting these contextual data traces for analysis and visualization. No explicit preprocessing steps such as filtering, calibration or validation are described in the source.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Physical to digital data are transmitted using the MQTT protocol. The source does not specify protocol versions, QoS levels, authentication mechanisms, or network topology details.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The twin creation follows a Model‑Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) methodology that explicitly subdivides the development effort across multiple abstraction layers: requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers. At each level the system is described using a variety of Modeling and Architecture Description Languages (ADLs). SysML is the primary language for representing architecture and behavior, with SysML v1.7 state‑machine diagrams and I‑Block Diagrams providing component‑and‑connector details and interface contracts. Complementary ADLs such as ACME, AADL, and MontiArc are employed to capture operational realities, especially in edge or embedded contexts; MontiArc Automaton is used for automated behavior specification. Behavioural models are subsequently simulated with MathWorks Simulink or equivalent simulation tools allowing verification of dynamic properties before code generation. The resulting behavioral artefacts are translated into executable artefacts in general‑purpose languages such as C++ or Java. Throughout this iterative process, models are integrated across heterogeneous notations, ensuring that active computational components that process incoming sensor streams are incorporated, thereby distinguishing the twin from a purely passive digital shadow.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The system conforms to established modelling and architecture standards, including the Unified Modeling Language (UML) for high‑level conceptual modelling and SysML for system architecture and behavior. Architecture Description Languages employed—ACME, AADL, and MontiArc—adhere to their respective specifications, enabling standardized component‑and‑connector descriptions and interface definitions that support interoperability across toolchains.

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction correctly identifies the MABI MAX 100 robot, its focus on the first vertical axis, and the specific components like the permanent magnet synchronous motor and cycloidal gearbox. It accurately describes the operational context (machining/manufacturing) and characteristics like repeatability. It could be expanded to include the specific controller model (Siemens SINUMERIK) or encoder details mentioned in the text. Supported by quotes: 'serial robot MABI MAX 100 with six axes from the Swiss company MABI Robotic AG', 'driven by a permanent magnet synchronous motor', 'suitable for complex manufacturing tasks such as machining'.

The extraction correctly identifies the service as a passive monitoring and diagnostics digital shadow. It accurately captures the 'automated one-way data flow', the 'multi-physics-aware identification' of friction, and the use of a 'dashboard' for visualization. While the specific user roles 'operators and maintenance engineers' are not explicitly named in the text (only 'stakeholders' are mentioned generally), the description of the service capabilities is well-grounded.

The extraction accurately describes the tools and enablers found in the text. It mentions 'Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) principles' and 'SysML', which are supported by the text: 'ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering modeling languages such as SysML [17]'. It lists architecture languages 'ACME, AADL and MontiArc', which are cited in Section II.A: 'ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26]'. The mention of 'MontiArc Automaton and SysML state machines' aligns with 'Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in SysML v1.7'. 'MathWorks Simulink' is cited as a simulation tool: 'combined with further (modeling) tools such as MathWorks Simulink'. 'Code generation utilizes C++ or Java' is supported by 'code in general-purpose languages like C++ or Java'. The reference to 'Digital Shadow reference model' and 'automated one-way data flow' matches Section II.B: 'A digital shadow is characterized by “an automated one-way data flow...' The extraction is highly specific, detailed, and grounded in the provided sources.

The document focuses on a single digital shadow for a specific robot axis and does not mention multiple instances, decentralized architectures, or coordination mechanisms between twins.

The extraction states 'Not in Document,' which is correct. The focus is on the internal loop between the robot and its shadow.
